frameworks.name: statistical learning of sensory regularities
frameworks.one_line: learned transitional regularities shape neural responses to expected stimuli

frameworks.name: predictive coding / sensory attenuation
frameworks.one_line: expectation-driven attenuation reframed as attention-gated rather than automatic

frameworks.name: attention as gain modulation
frameworks.one_line: attention required to express prediction-related response suppression

frameworks.name: probabilistic perceptual inference
frameworks.one_line: perception as integration of prior knowledge with sensory input

core_question: Does sensory attenuation following statistical learning occur automatically or require attention to the predictable stimuli?
key_assumptions: the first object can predict the identity of the second object after exposure
ventral visual stream BOLD reflects sensory response strength
attention can be directed away from predictable features
manipulations: stimulus expectedness (expected vs unexpected second object)
direction of attention (toward vs away from predictable objects)
key_findings: reliable attenuation of neural activity for expected vs unexpected stimuli in ventral visual stream
sensory attenuation only present when stimuli were attended
attenuation vanished when attention was directed away from the predictable objects
interpretations: statistical-learning attenuation is not automatic but depends on attention
neurocomputational theories of prior-likelihood integration must accommodate attentional gating
